speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape eight. Today is October 23, 1996. And we're interviewing Estelle Laughlin. You were telling me about being in Kielce, after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So my-- my mother and I-- we left my sister. Then-- and I-- I-- I am really leaving a lot out. But-- but time will not permit to tell other things that were equally-- equally outstanding in my-- equally-- equally memorable. My mother and I went ahead to try to find our cousins who lived in [INAUDIBLE], that the two cousins that we met in camp. The reason why my sister did not go with us was that we were afraid of losing that little plot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was also still in January. Or it was very, very cold. And we, my mother and I, we took the train. But from the train to Jarocin, we had to go through various small communities.

speaker: interviewee
text: And night was falling. And it was extremely cold. We got a-- a ride from-- from a peasant. But then he dropped us off. Now, we knew if we will not find shelter we'll freeze and die. I remember crossing the trestles and sliding. And that was-- that was such a horrendous night. Because it was all white and all ice. And in distance you had these little farm houses. And every house, every farmhouse we came to, they sicced dogs on us. And finally, fortunately, one peasant, after we pleaded with him, let us sleep through the night in the-- in the-- in his barn. Eventually, we worked our way to Jarocin. Then-- and we stayed with our cousins. And one of my cousins went and picked up my sister. They stayed in the house that was owned by the parents. And-- and-- and but at this point, it was owned by Poles. And they were in-- they had two young sons who were in the Armia Kryova, which was a-- a-- like the Polish Partisans. And they were also fascists, the Armia Kryova wanted also-- they did not want to see the Jews returning. They did not want to give up what they plundered-- you know, what was left. But they let us stay in the house, up in the attic. 'Cause they knew that my uncle, was-- he was a jeweler. And they found some of the hidden gold. But they knew that-- they hoped that my cousins would lead them to what they could not find. As a matter of fact, once my cousin saw the mother of that family carry his mother's umbrella. And he very gently approached her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you know, I really have nothing left of my mother. This umbrella was my mother's. Could I just ask you to part with it and let me have it as a-- just one thing from my mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said OK. But I have to check with my husband. The next-- and my sons. And the next day she said, you never asked me. I don't know anything. That's not your mother's umbrella. I don't know what you're talking about. And left. Anyway, my cousins, in the meantime, made arrangements for us one-- one morning, to pick up-- and they did find some things. Like, some nails were made out of gold. And we were going to leave-- leave secretly. And the existence there, there was a small Jewish community that came back. You could count them, probably, only on one or two hands. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we-- this was my first experience after the war, where we could go out into the fields and play and experience freedom and-- with-- with my friends. And-- and just run in the fields. And-- and-- and just-- but every time we left the town and we came back, we were taunted by-- by-- by the young Polish kids. Well, one morning, at the crack of dawn, we-- we-- we dressed very silently. And we snuck out from-- from the attic, all the way down. And we knew when the train will leave. And we disappeared. They did not catch us. But that, too, was such a random luck thing. Because that same day, they had scheduled a riot, a pogrom. Now, my cousins, for sure, would have been shot. And-- because they wanted to claim-- they were a threat, that they would claim the houses back. So this was-- this was during the good times, after the war ended. And then we went to-- we moved closer and closer to the American sector. And eventually, we worked our way into Germany, established contact with my mother's brother and two sisters. And eventually, we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to the United States? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Either 1948, or 1947, or 1949. I don't remember. I should have looked it up. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to adjust here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes it was. It-- it-- again, it-- it's the sun and the moon. It's the dark and the light. There was a hope. There was a hope. Of course, we came on an army ship. And we-- it-- it was not-- you know-- it was-- but I remember on the ship, my sister said to-- we-- we made a pact. We said, let's not expect anything. This way we will not be disappointed. So we-- at least there was opportunity. But we came without the language. We came without a point of reference. We-- not only was there a culture shock. We had to even find our own identities. We were [INAUDIBLE]. We were strangers. We were-- it-- it was-- it was very, very difficult. But there always was a hope. My sister and I were working in a shop, in a garment factory, sewing buttons. My sister was a finisher. But there was opportunity. There was hope. I-- I enrolled-- my sister and I enrolled to-- my mother did not go to work. And for my mother, to make the adjustment, I think it was just too much. She just-- her adjustment was-- she-- she-- it was much, much more difficult. We were younger. We were more resilient. My sister and I attended in evening schools. Because we wanted to learn the language. I immediately enrolled to a-- a high school in the evening. Because that was in my, really, ignorance. Because I only had three years of formal education, some of the And the schooling, and I had in schools. But when I went to school in-- for people like me, I felt that they-- I was as dumb as they were. [LAUGHS] I-- I came home and I said, mom, I'm as dumb as they are. I can-- and they are as dumb as I am. And I can't learn anything. So I must make it harder for myself. And I enrolled into a high school. I didn't speak the language. It-- but that was a challenge. And what I used to do-- we lived on a top floor-- I used to ring the bell when I came back from work, for my mother to get the food on the table. Than I would dash up, eat the food, eat my dinner, and then run to the evening classes. But all of that, again, was a promise.

speaker: interviewer
text: You married and had a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I married and had a family. And then I-- when my second-- I have three sons, Steven, Andy, and Bobby. God bless them. [LAUGHS] And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --when Andy was just a few months old, Steven was going to a community-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --nursery school, and the teacher felt that I communicated well with children. My son said that's because I-- my oldest son, Steven, said that's-- he thinks that that's because I was not a child. So it was easy for me to go back and identify with children. I don't know how true it is. But I do love children. And I do communicate well with them. And so she said, oh, you should work with children. And-- and it seemed very unreal. But I did. She encouraged me to go and find out. I went to find out. We lived then, in Cleveland, Ohio. And I went to Western Reserve. And I was going to find out if they have like, teachers aides. And I didn't realize that I was talking to the Assistant Dean. And he said, oh, you don't have to settle for being an assistant, a-- a-- a teacher's aid. And I said but, you know, I had no education. And he said, well, he said, I spent a long time talking with you. Your experiences were in education. And I started to go to school, to college. And I was a teacher. And-- and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Do you have any message that you would like to give to our viewer? For your children and your grandchildren to see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] My message-- you mentioned my children and my grandchildren. I'm thinking, particularly, my grandchildren. Because they are the seed. They are the torch that moves on. And I-- when I think of messages, I think both of messages and issues. They kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --get mixed up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I think of the issues that come to mind, I'm thinking of the revisionists. It-- the offense, the revisionists who are denying this awful truth. The offense, the pain, the insult that they cause by the denial to the-- to the-- to those-- to the victims, to those who survived, and to all of humanity. It's horrendous. You see, to me, these people are very real. They are still alive. They are full of promise still. You know, once people enter your life, even if they are gone, they stay with you. I think the other day I pointed out to you, the cactus plants on my windows. They sometimes-- when I catch-- when I catch the glimmer of-- of sun, the life, it's them. I-- I-- my cousin had cactus plants on the window. I wasn't conscious why. But I-- once I had them lined up, I knew it was-- it was because this is where I saw her. I think maybe sometimes I may just, out of the blue, step maybe, on a blade of grass, and they return. And they return. And they grip me. They shake me to the very soul of my existence. And the pain is just-- just so great. And to have people deny it, I think, is very cruel. I also feel that it's extremely important to keep the truth and the facts alive. I also think that it's very important to recognize that nobody wins wars. That the skinheads-- I use skinheads. And I mean skinheads, not because their hair is shaved, but because I-- I-- I mean the ones who are fascists now too, and want to return to the teachings of Hitler-- they need to be reminded that Hitler really didn't accomplish anything for his people. 20 million, or close it, Germans died. Their cities were demolished. Whatever they plundered and stole, they couldn't even enjoy. It was the-- it was the-- the young people, the people were-- were sacrificed. The cities were demolished. And it's also important to make a distinction when people talk about the bombing of Dresden. I think it's important to make the distinction. When Dresden was bombed, it was during the time when Germans were killing people by the millions. They were cooking people. They were killing nations, whole nations, in Europe. And the bombing was not in order to enrich, to-- to plunder and-- and-- and to take our homes away, to destroy other countries. The Allies were bombing in order to save. And I think that when the bombs were dropped on-- on Dresden, it was in hope that the people would rebuild. And I think if there would be any sign of an attempt of the German people to stop the war, they would be only too happy to stop the bombing. And I think, like, for instance in Berlin, when all of Germany was practically invaded, and Hitler was still pleading with the Germans. He said, oh-- and this is history. It's, you know-- this-- this-- this is documented. He did tell the Germans that you were not worthy of me. And he told them not to surrender. And they did not surrender. Even-- they fought till the very, very last minute. So the bombings were not-- there's no comparison. There is no comparison. I also sometimes feel offended when I hear people making fine distinctions. Like they'll say, oh-- they quibble over six million Jews, maybe it was five million Jews. Maybe it was 4 and a 1/2. I don't know. I didn't count them. All I know, that when my sister went back to Warsaw after the war, there wasn't one building left that was habitable. There wasn't one soul that she found. There-- no-- nobody-- nobody that I have known in my city, that I lived all my life, that I lived there, none of them survived except my mother and my sister and myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would like to tell to these people that if they don't believe me, let them go to every city and every shtetl in-- in-- in Poland. And let them count up and see how many survivors, Jewish survivors, they can find. And if that's not enough, let them go to every city and every shtetl, and-- and everywhere in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let them see. Let them count and see how many survived. Maybe not six million. Maybe five million.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But where are they? I feel that there's cruelty in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Above all, I-- I-- I'm-- I'm just wondering, a thought is rushing through my mind. Why am I sharing this? Why am I subjecting myself to reliving that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the sun is outside, just glowing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe I'm doing it partially because suffering needs witness. It needs acknowledgment. Maybe. Maybe only then you can let go. But above all, I feel that if any injustice is done, all humanity is left with the legacy. I am not the only witness. All of humanity is a witness. I think the offenders-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't-- it's-- I-- I-- I-- it's not guilt that I want from them. I don't need guilt. I don't-- I don't want them to turn into victims either. I don't want anyone to be a victim. But I do feel that it's important to understand. Because for the world to remain a good place for everyone, it has to be humane.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for doing interview with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you introduce us to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Charles Laughlin. And he has tears in his eyes now. Because right before the camera started, I ask him how would-- how is he affected by the fact that he's married to a person with my experience? And your response was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it breaks me up every time I think about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like when I go to the Holocaust Museum, I stand there and cry. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I'm touched by your--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's ridiculous.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am very touched. And I need your compassion and your understanding. My husband is not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I said before, that all of humanity is-- is tarnished by events like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a picture of my father, my sister, and myself. My sister is the taller one, on the right. And I am the shorter one, on the left. This picture was given to us after the war by our American relatives. Because we did not have anything at all, left from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would have-- I probably wasn't more than maybe seven, eight, eight years old here. It-- I don't know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin, Josef, and his wife, Techa. She's the one who had these cactus plants on her window. She is the one who soothed me when my-- when I imagined my father returning during the bombardments. He was an editor of a Jewish newspaper, 'The Bund.'

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- must have been shortly before the war. Because they got married right before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin, Dudek. He is the brother of Josef. And he was everyone's idol, because he was so bright. He was one of the very few Jews who entered higher education, that were permitted. Because he was so bright. And whatever he did-- this is a restored picture. And it's not a very good picture. But whatever he did, he did well. He was an athlete. He did everything wonderfully.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was taken before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken before the war. And I don't know exactly when. This is a picture of my mother in the United States. My mother is in the center of the picture. And the two other women are my cousins. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The little one is-- oh, no. He's not. OK. Can you tell me who these people are?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my children. On the right is my son, Bob, my youngest son. The one standing next to him is Steven, my oldest son. The one in the center is Andy, my middle son. Next to him is his wife. Next to his wife is the wife of Steven, Chris. Next-- right in front, the one sitting on the ground is Jenny. And these are my wonderful grandchildren. There is Alex and [? Janice ?] [? Ladd. ?] There is Matthew, his brother, in the large t-shirt. There is Twain, who is another child of Bobby. All three boys that I mentioned are Bobby's. The one in the back with the football, he's a wonderful athlete and a wonderful scholar. That's our Eric. And he's-- his sister, Lauren. She's my oldest granddaughter. And I'm so pleased that I have a chance to mention their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Nina. This is my youngest granddaughter. And she's now oh, like, maybe 19 months old. And she's just precious.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very proud of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This book, 'Bearing the Unbearable,' is a book written by my sister. I mentioned, earlier, that even when we didn't have enough bread, we composed poetry. And really, there was a lot written during the war. And even in-- in concentration camps, on the-- in the-- on the barracks, we-- we sang. We composed. It really sweetened our-- and-- and kindled our lives. She collected poetry written in Yiddish and Polish, translated them and also added commentary. And if you flip-- this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Freida Aaron. She lives in-- in New York. She's a professor. She's teaching comparative literature. And she's a very special person. She has two wonderful daughters. And-- and I have-- and they have-- she has wonderful grandchildren. And they are as close to me as my children are. And I should have had pictures of them too. But time doesn't allow. I would also like to mention one-- my-- my husband's-- my husband's grandson, Jeremy. And unfortunately, I omitted, too, his picture. But he's-- he's very important to me too.
